briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for March 29, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Urgent Climate Disasters and Deepening Crisis
sections.content: Amid warnings that the impact of the climate crisis depends on the speed of greenhouse gas reduction [2], climate change-induced disasters are intensifying both domestically and internationally. In 2025, climate disasters included the largest wildfires on record and the most severe drought in 108 years. Gwangju Metropolitan City, nicknamed 'Gwangfrica,' is experiencing a crisis due to extreme heat [3, 4]. Internationally, warnings have emerged that the 'double threat' of climate change and El Niño is pushing the Earth 'beyond its limits' [51], and extreme weather events are becoming a reality, with small-scale farmers in Hawaii beginning recovery efforts after deadly floods [58]. Scientists have published shocking research on the impact of climate change on wild grasslands [45], and analysis suggests that modern agriculture is also collapsing due to climate change [49].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Implementation Efforts and Policy Public Discourse
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to achieve carbon neutrality goals, with a particular emphasis on the need for public discourse regarding the revision of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality [10]. Suwon Special City is promoting the establishment of an integrated platform for a carbon-neutral green city [6, 26], while Asan City is leading green building initiatives by converting sports facility construction to carbon-neutral architecture [11, 13]. Citizen participation is also active, with carbon neutrality practice activities taking place at Mapo Environmental School [7, 23], and the second phase of 'Bitgoeul Net-Zero Friends' launched in Gwangju, encouraging citizens to participate in carbon neutrality practices [15]. Furthermore, Daegu City and Incheon City are intensively reviewing their 2030 greenhouse gas reduction implementation tasks and 2045 carbon neutrality execution capabilities, respectively, to strengthen their ability to achieve targets [18, 20]. Notable changes are also observed across industries, including discussions on a carbon-neutral direct payment system for the fisheries sector to support sustainable fishing villages, and the pace of carbon-neutral responses in the construction sector [1, 14, 17, 22].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: Efforts to transition to renewable energy continue both domestically and internationally, yet discussions on energy security and policy direction are also active. Concerns have been raised that renewable energy policies might 'U-turn' for energy security due to the outbreak of the Middle East conflict [28], and civil society has urged reforms in grid operation centered on renewable energy [31]. Nevertheless, Woori Financial Group is making a bold move by investing in renewable energy, including offshore wind power [29]. Various local governments, such as Gwangju Metropolitan City and Jeollabuk-do, are expanding renewable energy adoption through support programs for residential new and renewable energy installation costs [30, 35, 38, 41]. In Haenam County, a public-private consultative body has been launched for a 'Renewable Energy Self-Sufficient City' [33], and Gyeonggi Province is actively promoting community-based renewable energy transitions by holding resident briefings for 'Sunshine Income Villages,' where fishing village residents become owners of solar power plants [37]. Meanwhile, the emergence of AI data centers is putting big tech companies' clean energy transition efforts to the test [46], and there is a call for in-depth discussions on the 'carbon neutrality' conditions in the power sector and solutions to the energy trilemma [9, 12, 25].

sections.title: 🌍 Widespread Impacts of Climate Change and International Responses
sections.content: Climate change is impacting the world in various ways, bringing changes to daily life, such as the reshaping of Arctic rivers [52] and an earlier start to allergy seasons due to increased pollen [48]. Amidst this, expectations for nature-based solutions are growing, with research indicating that Everglades restoration can help mitigate the effects of climate change [47]. Impacts in the industrial sector are also becoming visible, with climate change cited as a reason for refinery closures [55, 57], and experts continue to share insights on the role of artificial intelligence in climate change [50]. On a somewhat positive note, predictions suggest that the 2026 hurricane season in the North Atlantic may be calmer due to falling sea temperatures [5].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the diverse domestic and international response efforts that are converging. We anticipate that these efforts will serve as a crucial foundation for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: ‘I’ve never seen anything like it’: Hawaii’s small farmers begin recovery after catastrophic flooding
articles.description: Two kona low storms dumped up to 50in of rain on Oahu, flooding fields and submerging equipmentEddie Oroyan’s farm was thriving when the storms hit. He and his wife had started LewaTerra Farm last year on a gorgeous stretch of land on the north shore of Oahu. They were delivering vegetables to customers in the community, selling at farmer’s markets and to local restaurants.Then, on the week of 10 March, a first kona low storm hit the island, bringing copious amounts of water, flooding their land

summary.phenomenon: In the week of March 10th, two Kona Low storms dumped up to 50 inches of rain on Oahu Island, Hawaii, flooding agricultural fields and submerging equipment.
summary.cause: Two Kona Low storms brought copious amounts of water, causing floods.
summary.outlook: Following devastating floods, small-scale farmers in Hawaii are beginning their recovery efforts.

articles.original_description: Two kona low storms dumped up to 50in of rain on Oahu, flooding fields and submerging equipmentEddie Oroyan’s farm was thriving when the storms hit. He and his wife had started LewaTerra Farm last year on a gorgeous stretch of land on the north shore of Oahu. They were delivering vegetables to customers in the community, selling at farmer’s markets and to local restaurants.Then, on the week of 10 March, a first kona low storm hit the island, bringing copious amounts of water, flooding their land
